text:
This article examines how international educational integration, primarily driven by the Bologna and Copenhagen processes, influences the reform of national professional education systems. The authors categorize these integration efforts across multiple dimensions: statutory regulation, institutional missions, organizational structures, quality assurance mechanisms, and the modernization of training content and management. 

Focusing on the Russian context, the study identifies the specific competitive advantages and disadvantages of the national market for educational services. It further evaluates the pedagogical, social, and economic benefits—as well as the inherent risks—of aligning professional education with international standards. Additionally, the paper analyzes the socio-economic impacts of hosting foreign students in Russian universities. These findings provide practical guidance for international cooperation departments seeking to integrate Russian vocational schools into a unified global educational space. The results also serve as a resource for developing training programs and teaching aids for university administrators navigating the complexities of global educational reform.
